summary: O'Neil argues that data mining work divides into two political categories: those who cause things to happen (startups, political campaigns) and those who prevent things from happening (corporations, government). Startups feel innovative and disruptive, creating an exciting culture even when doing banal work like ad optimization. Corporations become increasingly risk-averse and focused on 'covering your ass' as they grow larger. Government data miners are the most conservative, obsessed with preventing terrorist attacks rather than creating positive outcomes. This framework explains why Obama's campaign data miners left for startups rather than staying in government, and why Facebook's defensive posture aligns naturally with NSA collaboration.
key_points: Data mining divides politically into 'causing things to happen' versus 'preventing things from happening'
Startups cultivate a disruptive, innovative culture even when doing mundane work like ad optimization
Corporate environments become increasingly risk-averse and ass-covering as companies grow, stifling innovation
Government data miners are the most conservative, focused entirely on threat prevention rather than positive creation
This framework explains talent flows: Obama's data miners moved to startups, and Facebook's defensive corporate posture aligns with NSA interests
money_quotes: The world of data mining is divided into two broad categories: people who want to cause things to happen and people who want to prevent things from happening.

In start-ups, irrespective of what you're actually doing (what you're actually doing is probably incredibly banal, like getting people to click on ads), you feel like you're the first person ever to do it.
You feel like a motherfucker, and it feels great!
I've worked in enough companies to know that 'covering your ass' is a real thing, and can overwhelm a given company's other goals.
The larger the company, the more the fear sets in and the more time is spent covering one's ass and less time is spent inventing and staying state-of-the-art.
They got lots of people to allow access to their private information based on their trust of Obama, and they mined the fuck out of it.
It's all about preventing bad things from happening, and that makes for an atmosphere where causing good things to happen takes a rear seat.
It also makes sense that Facebook, which spends its time making sure no other social network grows enough to compete with it, works so well with the NSA.
key_insight: The political culture of data mining organizations—whether innovative or defensive—is shaped not by their technical work but by whether they're trying to cause change or prevent it.
